title: 沉睡谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\140-沉睡谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
During a discussion regarding the death of Helen Kennedy, Jervis proposes a hypothesis to explain the discrepancies in the case. He suggests that Calvin Harriott did not kill his wife, but was induced to believe he did. Jervis posits that an unknown individual, referred to as "X," anaesthetized Harriott with a high concentration of anesthetic in his whisky. While Harriott was unconscious, X strangled Helen in the hall, moved her to a bed to simulate a love affair, and then moved the body. This theory accounts for Harriott's belief that he committed the crime after waking up in a state of confusion and jealousy.

The group discusses potential motives for X, focusing on men from Helen's past. Miss Marple reveals that Helen had previously been engaged to a lawyer named Walter Feen, whom she traveled abroad to marry before breaking the engagement. Miss Marple, noting her own habit of gathering local gossip, mentions that Feen is now a senior partner at a law firm and lives with his mother. Gwendolyn suggests that Helen may have encountered another man while traveling abroad, possibly a married man of social standing. Miss Marple supports this, suggesting that a passionate but impossible romance could have caused Helen to break her engagement to Feen and return home. Gwendolyn concludes that Helen's subsequent relationship with her father was not a passionate affair, but a mutual consolation between two people sharing personal troubles.
